title: "Binance Gold & Silver Perpetuals and ETF Markets"
author: "KSE Python course project"
format:
  html:
    df-print: paged
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=2.2cm
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{etoolbox}
        \AtBeginEnvironment{longtable}{\small}
        \AtBeginEnvironment{tabular}{\small}
        \setlength{\LTcapwidth}{\textwidth}
        \setlength{\LTleft}{0pt}
        \setlength{\LTright}{0pt}
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## 1. Introduction and research question

**Research question.**  
Did the introduction of Binance TRADFI gold and silver perpetual futures change volatility, liquidity and price integration in the corresponding precious metal ETF markets (GLD and SLV)?

**Why this story matters.** Gold and silver have long been traded via physical-backed ETFs (GLD, SLV) on regulated exchanges. In early 2026, Binance listed 24/7 tradable perpetuals on the same underlyings (XAUUSDT, XAGUSDT). That creates a second venue for exposure to the same economic assets. The natural question is whether the incumbent ETF markets *changed*—became more volatile, more liquid, or more tightly linked to the new venue. This report tells that story: we measure what happened in GLD and SLV *after* the launch and interpret the results in terms of market structure and implications for investors and regulators.

**Main hypotheses (testable with pre/post data).**

- **H1 (volatility)**: Realized volatility of gold and silver ETFs increased after the launch of Binance XAUUSDT and XAGUSDT TRADFI perpetual futures.
- **H2 (liquidity)**: Trading activity and liquidity of GLD and SLV improved (higher volume, lower Amihud illiquidity) after the launch.

We do *not* formulate a formal hypothesis on “integration increased”, because Binance returns do not exist before the launch, so we cannot compute pre-event GLD–XAU or SLV–XAG correlation or beta. We still report post-event rolling correlation and integration metrics as **descriptive** evidence (how strongly ETF and perpetual co-move after the launch), but we do not test a pre/post change for integration.

**How we identify the impact on the market.** We use an **event study** with (i) pre/post comparison of means and t-tests, and (ii) **OLS regressions** where the outcome is volatility (or liquidity proxy) and the key regressor is a **post-event dummy** (`post_event` = 1 after the launch date), plus controls (DXY, VIX, 10Y yield changes). The coefficient on `post_event` is our estimate of the change in the outcome after the launch, conditional on controls. We do not have a control group (e.g. other assets without a new derivative), so we do not use difference-in-differences; the design is a single-market before/after event study with controls. See `Literature/LITERATURE_METHODS.md` for a comparison with the Bitcoin futures paper (Augustin et al., Management Science 2023), which uses DiD.

### 1.1 How we approach the task

- **Data.** We collect daily prices and returns for ETFs (GLD, SLV) and Binance perpetuals (XAUUSDT, XAGUSDT), plus controls (DXY, VIX, 10Y yield). From these we build a daily panel and metrics: realized volatility (20d, 10d), Parkinson, GK, volume, Amihud, and ETF-Binance correlation and beta (after the launch).
- **Event.** The event is the launch date of Binance TRADFI XAUUSDT/XAGUSDT (**2026-01-05**). Before that date we have no Binance data, so we estimate integration (correlation, beta) only after the event.
- **Method.** (1) Pre/post comparison of means and Welch t-tests for volatility and liquidity. (2) OLS regressions: outcome is volatility or liquidity, regressors include a `post_event` dummy plus controls (DXY, VIX, 10Y). The coefficient on `post_event` is the effect of the launch conditional on macro controls. We use winsorization and HAC (Newey-West) standard errors.
- **Interpretation.** A positive and significant coefficient on `post_event` in the volatility regression supports H1; higher volume and lower Amihud after the event support H2. We describe integration (GLD-XAU, SLV-XAG correlation) only after the event, with no formal hypothesis test (no pre-period for Binance).

## 2. Data and feature set

We use three pre‑built datasets produced by `build_features.py`:

- **Daily panel** (`features_daily.csv`): 118 trading days, 87 variables. Used for the event study (pre/post comparison, regressions).
- **Hourly panel** (`features_hourly.csv`): one row per hour over the same calendar span; Binance 1m aggregated to hourly, ETF/controls forward-filled from daily.
- **1-minute panel** (`features_1m.csv`): one row per Binance XAUUSDT 1m candle with daily ETF/controls merged by date. Used for intraday analysis and high-frequency metrics.

Raw data sources and variable definitions are in `DATA_DESCRIPTION.md`, `VARIABLES_DESCRIPTION.md`, and `DATA_ROW_COUNTS_AND_PERIODS.md`. A mapping of which variables are used where (and how to extend usage) is in `VARIABLE_USAGE.md`.

### 2.2 How we use 1-minute data

We use minute-level data in **two ways**:

1. **Panel `features_1m.csv` (Binance 1m + daily merge).**  
   Built from Binance XAUUSDT 1m klines: each row is one minute candle; ETF and control variables are merged from the daily panel by **date** (same value for all minutes of that day). We use this panel to analyse intraday behaviour of the perpetual: 1m returns and their distribution, and daily realized volatility from 1m returns (see section 3.1.1).

2. **Minute-level correlation (last 7 days).**  
   Yahoo provides 1m data for GLD/SLV only for the **last ~7 days**. Where that overlaps with Binance 1m, we align both by datetime and compute **minute-level return correlation** (GLD–XAUUSDT and SLV–XAGUSDT) over the same 1m bars. That gives a same-frequency measure of short-run co-movement (section 3.2, “Minute-level correlation”). So: **1m data from Binance** feeds the large panel and intraday stats; **1m data from Yahoo (last 7d)** feeds the minute-level correlation with ETFs.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import os

# Plot theme: clean grid, readable fonts, consistent palette
plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid")
plt.rcParams["figure.dpi"] = 120
plt.rcParams["font.size"] = 10
plt.rcParams["axes.titlesize"] = 12
plt.rcParams["axes.labelsize"] = 10
COLORS = {
    "gld": "#B8860B",      # dark goldenrod
    "slv": "#708090",       # slate gray
    "xau": "#C9A227",      # Binance gold (amber, distinct from GLD)
    "xag": "#0D9488",      # Binance silver (teal, distinct from SLV grey)
    "event": "#C41E3A",    # crimson
    "pre": "#2E86AB",      # blue
    "post": "#E94F37",     # red-orange
}

# Data paths: support both project layout and Colab (processed/ at root)
proc_dir = "data/processed" if os.path.exists("data/processed") else "processed"
raw_dir = "data/raw" if os.path.exists("data/raw") else "raw"
if not os.path.exists(proc_dir):
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"Processed data folder not found. Create {proc_dir} with features_daily.csv, features_hourly.csv, features_1m.csv")

# Row counts for all three panels
for name, f in [("Daily", "features_daily.csv"), ("Hourly", "features_hourly.csv"), ("1-minute", "features_1m.csv")]:
    path = os.path.join(proc_dir, f)
    if os.path.exists(path):
        n = sum(1 for _ in open(path)) - 1
        print(f"{name}: {n} rows ({f})")
```

```{python}
# Load daily panel for event study (main analysis)
path_daily = os.path.join(proc_dir, "features_daily.csv")
df = pd.read_csv(path_daily, parse_dates=["Date"])
df = df.set_index("Date").sort_index()

# Validate: required columns for the analysis
required = ["px_gld_close", "px_slv_close", "ret_gld", "ret_slv"]
missing = [c for c in required if c not in df.columns]
if missing:
    raise KeyError(f"Daily panel missing columns: {missing}. Check build_features.py output.")
print(f"Range: {df.index.min().date()} to {df.index.max().date()}, {len(df)} rows × {len(df.columns)} columns.")
df.shape
```

First rows of the daily panel (first 8 of 87 columns shown so the table fits on the page in PDF).

```{python}
# Show first 8 columns so the table fits on the page (PDF); full panel has 87 columns
_n_cols = min(8, len(df.columns))
df.head().iloc[:, : _n_cols]
```

### 2.1 Basic cleaning and event date

We define the **event** as the **launch** of Binance XAUUSDT TRADFI perpetual futures. So there is one date: 2026‑01‑05 (event = launch).  
To keep a meaningful pre‑event window, we only require that ETF prices and returns are available; Binance‑specific fields are allowed to be missing before the launch.

```{python}
event_date = pd.Timestamp("2026-01-05")

# Require only ETF prices/returns (pre‑event we do not have Binance data)
core_cols = ["px_gld_close", "px_slv_close", "ret_gld", "ret_slv"]
df_core = df.dropna(subset=core_cols).copy()

df_core["post_event"] = (df_core.index >= event_date).astype(int)

# Compact summary for PDF (avoids wide .info() overflow); full .info() in HTML
print(f"Range: {df_core.index.min().date()} to {df_core.index.max().date()}, {len(df_core)} rows × {len(df_core.columns)} columns.")
df_core.dtypes.head(12)
```

```{python}
df_core[["post_event"]].value_counts().sort_index()
```

## 3. Descriptive statistics

### 3.1 Prices and returns

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11, 6), sharex=True)
for ax in axes:
    ax.axvspan(event_date, df_core.index.max(), alpha=0.08, color=COLORS["event"], zorder=0)
    ax.axvline(event_date, color=COLORS["event"], linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label="Binance TRADFI launch")

# ETF prices
axes[0].plot(df_core.index, df_core["px_gld_close"], color=COLORS["gld"], linewidth=1.8, label="GLD")
axes[0].plot(df_core.index, df_core["px_slv_close"], color=COLORS["slv"], linewidth=1.8, label="SLV")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Price (USD)")
axes[0].set_title("ETF closing prices (GLD, SLV)")
axes[0].legend(loc="upper left")

# Binance daily close
axes[1].plot(df_core.index, df_core["px_xau_binance_close"], color=COLORS["xau"], linewidth=1.8, label="XAUUSDT")
axes[1].plot(df_core.index, df_core["px_xag_binance_close"], color=COLORS["xag"], linewidth=1.8, label="XAGUSDT")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Price (USD)")
axes[1].set_title("Binance TRADFI perpetuals (daily close)")
axes[1].legend(loc="upper left")

fig.suptitle("Price series and event window", fontsize=13, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Cumulative returns:** ETFs (GLD, SLV) from the first date, index 100 at start. Binance (XAUUSDT, XAGUSDT) only from the event date—we have no Binance prices before the launch; their index is 100 at the event.

```{python}
# ETFs: full sample, index 100 at start. Binance: from event_date only (no data before).
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4))
for col, lab, c in [("ret_gld", "GLD", COLORS["gld"]), ("ret_slv", "SLV", COLORS["slv"])]:
    if col not in df_core.columns:
        continue
    r = df_core[col].fillna(0)
    cum = (1 + r).cumprod()
    cum = 100 * cum / cum.iloc[0]
    ax.plot(cum.index, cum, label=lab, color=c, linewidth=1.8)
for col, lab, c in [("ret_xau_binance", "XAUUSDT", COLORS["xau"]), ("ret_xag_binance", "XAGUSDT", COLORS["xag"])]:
    if col not in df_core.columns:
        continue
    post = df_core.loc[df_core.index >= event_date, col].dropna()
    if post.empty:
        continue
    cum = (1 + post).cumprod()
    cum = 100 * cum / cum.iloc[0]
    ax.plot(cum.index, cum, label=lab, color=c, linewidth=1.8)
ax.axvspan(event_date, df_core.index.max(), alpha=0.08, color=COLORS["event"], zorder=0)
ax.axvline(event_date, color=COLORS["event"], linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label="Event")
ax.set_ylabel("Cumulative return (index = 100)")
ax.set_title("Cumulative returns: ETFs vs Binance perpetuals (Binance from launch only)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", ncol=2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Post-event integration in one picture:** scatter of ETF daily returns vs Binance perpetual returns (post-event only). Points along the diagonal mean the two move together; the regression line and correlation summarize the link.

```{python}
post_mask = df_core["post_event"] == 1
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))
# GLD vs XAU
g = df_core.loc[post_mask, ["ret_gld", "ret_xau_binance"]].dropna()
if len(g) > 5:
    axes[0].scatter(g["ret_xau_binance"], g["ret_gld"], alpha=0.6, s=25, color=COLORS["gld"], edgecolors="white", linewidths=0.3)
    z = np.polyfit(g["ret_xau_binance"], g["ret_gld"], 1)
    xl = np.array([g["ret_xau_binance"].min(), g["ret_xau_binance"].max()])
    axes[0].plot(xl, np.poly1d(z)(xl), color=COLORS["event"], linestyle="--", linewidth=2, label=f"Fit (ρ = {g.corr().iloc[0,1]:.3f})")
    axes[0].legend()
    axes[0].set_xlabel("XAUUSDT daily return")
    axes[0].set_ylabel("GLD daily return")
    axes[0].set_title("GLD vs XAUUSDT (post-event)")
    axes[0].axhline(0, color="gray", linestyle=":", alpha=0.7)
    axes[0].axvline(0, color="gray", linestyle=":", alpha=0.7)
# SLV vs XAG
s = df_core.loc[post_mask, ["ret_slv", "ret_xag_binance"]].dropna()
if len(s) > 5:
    axes[1].scatter(s["ret_xag_binance"], s["ret_slv"], alpha=0.6, s=25, color=COLORS["slv"], edgecolors="white", linewidths=0.3)
    z2 = np.polyfit(s["ret_xag_binance"], s["ret_slv"], 1)
    xl2 = np.array([s["ret_xag_binance"].min(), s["ret_xag_binance"].max()])
    axes[1].plot(xl2, np.poly1d(z2)(xl2), color=COLORS["event"], linestyle="--", linewidth=2, label=f"Fit (ρ = {s.corr().iloc[0,1]:.3f})")
    axes[1].legend()
    axes[1].set_xlabel("XAGUSDT daily return")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("SLV daily return")
    axes[1].set_title("SLV vs XAGUSDT (post-event)")
    axes[1].axhline(0, color="gray", linestyle=":", alpha=0.7)
    axes[1].axvline(0, color="gray", linestyle=":", alpha=0.7)
plt.suptitle("Daily returns: ETF vs Binance perpetual (post-event)", fontsize=12, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**How the distribution of returns changed:** pre- vs post-event density of daily returns. A wider (more dispersed) distribution after the event is consistent with higher volatility.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))
for ax, ret_col, label, c in zip(axes, ["ret_gld", "ret_slv"], ["GLD", "SLV"], [COLORS["gld"], COLORS["slv"]]):
    pre_ret = df_core.loc[df_core["post_event"] == 0, ret_col].dropna()
    post_ret = df_core.loc[df_core["post_event"] == 1, ret_col].dropna()
    if len(pre_ret) > 2 and len(post_ret) > 2:
        pre_ret.plot(kind="kde", ax=ax, label="Pre-event", color=COLORS["pre"], linewidth=2)
        post_ret.plot(kind="kde", ax=ax, label="Post-event", color=COLORS["post"], linewidth=2)
        ax.set_xlabel("Daily return")
        ax.set_title(f"{label} daily returns")
        ax.legend()
        lo = min(pre_ret.min(), post_ret.min()) - 0.01
        hi = max(pre_ret.max(), post_ret.max()) + 0.01
        ax.set_xlim(lo, hi)
plt.suptitle("Return distribution: pre vs post event", fontsize=12, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Python analytics: interactive visualization (Plotly)** — as in the class examples (e.g. Debt vs Renew), we use Plotly for one interactive chart: hover over dates to see values.

```{python}
try:
    import plotly.express as px
    import plotly.graph_objects as go
    # ETFs: full sample. Binance: from event only (no data before launch).
    cum_parts = []
    for col, name in [("ret_gld", "GLD"), ("ret_slv", "SLV")]:
        if col not in df_core.columns:
            continue
        r = df_core[col].fillna(0)
        cum = (1 + r).cumprod()
        cum = 100 * cum / cum.iloc[0]
        cum_parts.append(cum.rename(name).reset_index().melt(id_vars="Date", var_name="Series", value_name="Index"))
    for col, name in [("ret_xau_binance", "XAUUSDT"), ("ret_xag_binance", "XAGUSDT")]:
        if col not in df_core.columns:
            continue
        post = df_core.loc[df_core.index >= event_date, col].dropna()
        if post.empty:
            continue
        cum = (1 + post).cumprod()
        cum = 100 * cum / cum.iloc[0]
        cum_parts.append(cum.rename(name).reset_index().melt(id_vars="Date", var_name="Series", value_name="Index"))
    cum_long = pd.concat(cum_parts, ignore_index=True) if cum_parts else pd.DataFrame()
    if not cum_long.empty:
        fig_px = px.line(cum_long, x="Date", y="Index", color="Series",
                         title="Cumulative returns (index=100) — Binance from launch only",
                         labels={"Index": "Cumulative return index"})
        fig_px.add_vline(x=event_date, line_dash="dash", line_color=COLORS["event"])
        fig_px.update_layout(height=400)
        fig_px.show()
except Exception as e:
    print("Plotly not available:", e)
```

**Time-series diagnostics (statsmodels):** ACF of daily returns to show short-run dependence, as in the class time-series examples (e.g. AutoReg/VAR project).

```{python}
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
ret_gld_clean = df_core["ret_gld"].dropna()
ret_slv_clean = df_core["ret_slv"].dropna()
plot_acf(ret_gld_clean, ax=axes[0], lags=min(30, len(ret_gld_clean)//2 - 1), alpha=0.05)
axes[0].set_title("ACF: GLD daily returns")
plot_acf(ret_slv_clean, ax=axes[1], lags=min(30, len(ret_slv_clean)//2 - 1), alpha=0.05)
axes[1].set_title("ACF: SLV daily returns")
plt.suptitle("Autocorrelation of returns (statsmodels)", fontsize=12, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

A concise list of Python techniques used in this project (pandas, numpy, scipy, statsmodels, Plotly, etc.), aligned with the course References, is in `PYTHON_TECHNIQUES.md`.

```{python}
returns_cols = ["ret_gld", "ret_slv", "ret_xau_binance", "ret_xag_binance"]
df_core[returns_cols].describe().T[["mean", "std", "min", "max"]]
```

#### 3.1.2 Intraday analysis using the 1-minute panel (`features_1m`)

We use the **1-minute panel** here (not only for the row count): we load `features_1m.csv`, take 1m returns of XAUUSDT (`ret_xau_binance`), and show **(a)** the distribution of 1m returns and **(b)** daily realized volatility computed from 1m returns (annualized). So the minute data directly feeds intraday volatility and return shape.

```{python}
path_1m = os.path.join(proc_dir, "features_1m.csv")
if os.path.exists(path_1m):
    df_1m = pd.read_csv(path_1m, parse_dates=["open_time"], index_col="open_time")
    if "ret_xau_binance" in df_1m.columns:
        ret_1m = df_1m["ret_xau_binance"].dropna()
        ret_1m = ret_1m[np.isfinite(ret_1m)]
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
        axes[0].hist(ret_1m, bins=80, color=COLORS["xau"], alpha=0.8, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3)
        axes[0].set_xlabel("XAUUSDT 1m log return")
        axes[0].set_ylabel("Count")
        axes[0].set_title("Distribution of 1m returns (features_1m)")
        # Daily realized vol from 1m (std of 1m returns per day, annualized)
        df_1m_local = df_1m.copy()
        df_1m_local["date"] = df_1m_local.index.date
        daily_vol_1m = df_1m_local.groupby("date")["ret_xau_binance"].std() * np.sqrt(24 * 60) * 100  # ann. % approx
        daily_vol_1m.plot(ax=axes[1], color=COLORS["xau"], linewidth=1.2)
        axes[1].set_xlabel("Date")
        axes[1].set_ylabel("Realized vol (1m, ann. % approx)")
        axes[1].set_title("Daily realized volatility from 1m returns")
        plt.suptitle("Use of 1-minute panel: returns and intraday volatility", fontsize=12, y=1.02)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()
    else:
        print("features_1m loaded but ret_xau_binance not found.")
else:
    print("features_1m.csv not found; run build_features.py after download_data.py.")
```

### 3.2 Volatility and liquidity over time

We look at 20‑day realized volatility for GLD and SLV and ETF trading volume.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(11, 8), sharex=True)
for ax in axes:
    ax.axvspan(event_date, df_core.index.max(), alpha=0.08, color=COLORS["event"], zorder=0)
    ax.axvline(event_date, color=COLORS["event"], linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5)

axes[0].plot(df_core.index, df_core["vol_realized_20_ret_gld"], color=COLORS["gld"], linewidth=1.5, label="GLD 20d")
axes[0].plot(df_core.index, df_core["vol_realized_20_ret_slv"], color=COLORS["slv"], linewidth=1.5, label="SLV 20d")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Volatility")
axes[0].set_title("Realized 20‑day volatility")
axes[0].legend()

axes[1].plot(df_core.index, df_core["vol_parkinson_gld"], color=COLORS["gld"], linewidth=1.5, label="GLD")
axes[1].plot(df_core.index, df_core["vol_parkinson_slv"], color=COLORS["slv"], linewidth=1.5, label="SLV")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Volatility")
axes[1].set_title("Parkinson volatility")
axes[1].legend()

if "vol_gld" in df_core.columns and "vol_slv" in df_core.columns:
    axes[2].plot(df_core.index, df_core["vol_gld"], color=COLORS["gld"], linewidth=1.5, label="GLD")
    axes[2].plot(df_core.index, df_core["vol_slv"], color=COLORS["slv"], linewidth=1.5, label="SLV")
    axes[2].set_ylabel("Volume")
    axes[2].set_title("ETF trading volume")
    axes[2].legend()

fig.suptitle("Volatility and volume over time", fontsize=13, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Why there is no pre-period for integration.** The event is the *launch* of Binance XAUUSDT/XAGUSDT. Before that date there are no Binance prices or returns, so series like `corr_20_gld_xau` and `beta_60_gld_on_xau` (which need both ETF and Binance returns) are undefined before the launch. We therefore **cannot** estimate “integration before the event” in the same way we do for volatility or liquidity: there is no pre-period for Binance–ETF co-movement. We can only describe integration *after* the launch (level and evolution of rolling correlation) and, in the pre/post table, the “pre” mean for correlation is effectively missing or based on no data.

**What this shows:** The *rolling 20-day correlation* is the correlation between daily returns of the ETF (GLD or SLV) and the Binance perpetual (XAUUSDT or XAGUSDT) over the last 20 days. Values near 1 mean the two series move together. This metric exists only when both sides have data (post-event). The chart and the pre/post table in section 4 show post-event integration only; there is no comparable pre-event integration measure.

**Single chart (gold and silver together):** Same scale and dates so both lines are easy to compare. Y-axis is limited to the range where the data lies so the lines are not squeezed at the top. The vertical line is the **event date** — that is, the **launch date** of Binance XAUUSDT (2026-01-05). We use this single date as “the event” everywhere in the report. The SLV–XAG line starts later than GLD–XAU because XAGUSDT was listed a few days after XAUUSDT, so the 20-day rolling window has enough data only from a later date.

```{python}
import matplotlib.dates as mdates

c_gld = df_core["corr_20_gld_xau"].dropna() if "corr_20_gld_xau" in df_core.columns else pd.Series(dtype=float)
c_slv = df_core["corr_20_slv_xag"].dropna() if "corr_20_slv_xag" in df_core.columns else pd.Series(dtype=float)
if c_gld.empty and c_slv.empty:
    print("No rolling correlation data available.")
else:
    all_dates = c_gld.index.union(c_slv.index)
    x_max = all_dates.max()
    # Include event date on the left so the event line is visible
    x_min = min(event_date, all_dates.min())
    # Focus y-axis on where the data is so lines are not squeezed at the top
    vals = pd.concat([c_gld, c_slv])
    y_lo = max(0.0, float(vals.min()) - 0.05)
    y_hi = min(1.02, float(vals.max()) + 0.03)
    if y_hi - y_lo < 0.15:
        y_lo = max(0.0, y_hi - 0.2)

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
    ax.axvline(event_date, color=COLORS["event"], linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label="Event = launch (2026-01-05)")
    ax.set_xlim(x_min, x_max)
    ax.set_ylim(y_lo, y_hi)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%Y-%m-%d"))
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.WeekdayLocator(interval=1))
    plt.setp(ax.xaxis.get_majorticklabels(), rotation=35, ha="right")
    if not c_gld.empty:
        ax.plot(c_gld.index, c_gld.values, color=COLORS["gld"], linewidth=2.2, label="GLD–XAU 20d")
    if not c_slv.empty:
        ax.plot(c_slv.index, c_slv.values, color=COLORS["slv"], linewidth=2.2, label="SLV–XAG 20d")
    ax.set_ylabel("Rolling 20‑day correlation")
    ax.set_title("Price integration: ETF vs Binance perpetual (post-event)")
    ax.legend(loc="lower right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

#### Minute-level correlation (last 7 days)

For the most recent 7 days we have *minute-level* data for both ETFs (Yahoo) and Binance. We merge on datetime and compute 1m returns to get a same-frequency measure of short-run co-movement.

```{python}
# Optional: minute-level GLD/SLV from Yahoo (last 7d) + Binance 1m overlap
yahoo_1m_path = os.path.join(raw_dir, "etf_gld_slv_1m_last7d.csv")
binance_xau_path = os.path.join(raw_dir, "binance_xauusdt_1m.csv")
binance_xag_path = os.path.join(raw_dir, "binance_xagusdt_1m.csv")

minute_corr_gld_xau = minute_corr_slv_xag = None
if os.path.exists(yahoo_1m_path) and os.path.exists(binance_xau_path) and os.path.exists(binance_xag_path):
    y1m = pd.read_csv(yahoo_1m_path, parse_dates=["datetime"])
    y1m = y1m.set_index("datetime").sort_index()
    if y1m.index.tz is None:
        y1m.index = y1m.index.tz_localize("UTC", ambiguous="infer")
    xau = pd.read_csv(binance_xau_path, parse_dates=["open_time"])
    xau = xau.set_index("open_time").sort_index()
    if xau.index.tz is None:
        xau.index = xau.index.tz_localize("UTC", ambiguous="infer")
    xag = pd.read_csv(binance_xag_path, parse_dates=["open_time"])
    xag = xag.set_index("open_time").sort_index()
    if xag.index.tz is None:
        xag.index = xag.index.tz_localize("UTC", ambiguous="infer")

    close_gld = y1m["gld_close"].dropna()
    close_slv = y1m["slv_close"].dropna()
    close_xau = xau["close"].rename("xau_close")
    close_xag = xag["close"].rename("xag_close")

    # Align to common index (inner join on datetime)
    j = close_gld.to_frame().join(close_xau, how="inner")
    j = j.dropna()
    if len(j) > 30:
        ret_gld_1m = np.log(j["gld_close"] / j["gld_close"].shift(1)).dropna()
        ret_xau_1m = np.log(j["xau_close"] / j["xau_close"].shift(1)).dropna()
        common = ret_gld_1m.index.intersection(ret_xau_1m.index)
        minute_corr_gld_xau = ret_gld_1m.loc[common].corr(ret_xau_1m.loc[common])

    j2 = close_slv.to_frame().join(close_xag, how="inner").dropna()
    if len(j2) > 30:
        ret_slv_1m = np.log(j2["slv_close"] / j2["slv_close"].shift(1)).dropna()
        ret_xag_1m = np.log(j2["xag_close"] / j2["xag_close"].shift(1)).dropna()
        common2 = ret_slv_1m.index.intersection(ret_xag_1m.index)
        minute_corr_slv_xag = ret_slv_1m.loc[common2].corr(ret_xag_1m.loc[common2])

if minute_corr_gld_xau is not None or minute_corr_slv_xag is not None:
    print("Minute-level return correlation (last 7 days, overlapping 1m bars):")
    if minute_corr_gld_xau is not None:
        print(f"  GLD–XAU: {minute_corr_gld_xau:.4f}")
    if minute_corr_slv_xag is not None:
        print(f"  SLV–XAG: {minute_corr_slv_xag:.4f}")
else:
    print("Minute-level overlap not available (run download_data.py to fetch etf_gld_slv_1m_last7d.csv).")
```

```{python}
liq_cols = []
for c in ["amihud_gld", "amihud_20_gld", "amihud_slv", "amihud_20_slv"]:
    if c in df_core.columns:
        liq_cols.append(c)

df_core[liq_cols].describe().T[["mean", "std", "min", "max"]]
```

### 3.3 Correlogram

We compute the correlation matrix for a set of variables that are meaningful for the analysis: returns, volatility measures, integration (rolling correlation/beta), liquidity proxies, controls and the event dummy. Only numeric series with sufficient non-missing overlap are included.

```{python}
# Variables that make sense for correlation (numeric, interpretable)
corr_candidates = [
    "ret_gld", "ret_slv", "ret_xau_binance", "ret_xag_binance",
    "vol_realized_20_ret_gld", "vol_realized_20_ret_slv",
    "vol_realized_20_ret_xau_binance", "vol_realized_20_ret_xag_binance",
    "vol_parkinson_gld", "vol_parkinson_slv",
    "vol_parkinson_xau_binance", "vol_parkinson_xag_binance",
    "corr_20_gld_xau", "corr_20_slv_xag",
    "beta_60_gld_on_xau", "beta_60_slv_on_xag",
    "vol_ratio_gld_xau", "vol_ratio_slv_xag",
    "log_vol_gld", "log_vol_slv",
    "amihud_gld", "amihud_slv", "amihud_20_gld", "amihud_20_slv",
    "dxy", "vix", "tnx", "dxy_ret", "vix_ret", "tnx_ret",
    "post_event", "funding_xau", "funding_xag",
]
corr_cols = [c for c in corr_candidates if c in df_core.columns]
corr_data = df_core[corr_cols].dropna(how="all", axis=1).dropna(how="any", axis=0)
if corr_data.shape[0] < 5:
    corr_data = df_core[corr_cols].dropna(how="all", axis=1)
    corr_data = corr_data.dropna(axis=0, thresh=corr_data.shape[1] // 2)
C = corr_data.corr()
```

```{python}
# Short labels for readability
short = {
    "ret_gld": "ret GLD", "ret_slv": "ret SLV",
    "ret_xau_binance": "ret XAU", "ret_xag_binance": "ret XAG",
    "vol_realized_20_ret_gld": "vol20 GLD", "vol_realized_20_ret_slv": "vol20 SLV",
    "vol_realized_20_ret_xau_binance": "vol20 XAU", "vol_realized_20_ret_xag_binance": "vol20 XAG",
    "vol_parkinson_gld": "Park GLD", "vol_parkinson_slv": "Park SLV",
    "vol_parkinson_xau_binance": "Park XAU", "vol_parkinson_xag_binance": "Park XAG",
    "corr_20_gld_xau": "corr GLD-XAU", "corr_20_slv_xag": "corr SLV-XAG",
    "beta_60_gld_on_xau": "beta GLD/XAU", "beta_60_slv_on_xag": "beta SLV/XAG",
    "vol_ratio_gld_xau": "vol ratio G/X", "vol_ratio_slv_xag": "vol ratio S/X",
    "log_vol_gld": "log vol GLD", "log_vol_slv": "log vol SLV",
    "amihud_gld": "Amihud GLD", "amihud_slv": "Amihud SLV",
    "amihud_20_gld": "Amihud20 GLD", "amihud_20_slv": "Amihud20 SLV",
    "dxy": "DXY", "vix": "VIX", "tnx": "TNX",
    "dxy_ret": "DXY ret", "vix_ret": "VIX ret", "tnx_ret": "TNX ret",
    "post_event": "post_event", "funding_xau": "fund XAU", "funding_xag": "fund XAG",
}
C_plot = C.rename(index=short, columns=short)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 12))
sns.heatmap(
    C_plot,
    cmap="RdBu_r",
    center=0,
    vmin=-1,
    vmax=1,
    square=True,
    linewidths=0.25,
    ax=ax,
    annot=False,
    cbar_kws={"shrink": 0.8},
)
ax.set_title("Correlogram: returns, volatility, integration, liquidity, controls", fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Summary: variables most correlated with post_event (if any)
if "post_event" in C.columns:
    post_corr = C["post_event"].drop("post_event", errors="ignore")
    post_corr = post_corr.reindex(post_corr.abs().sort_values(ascending=False).index)
    post_corr.head(12)
```

**Comment.** Variables with the largest absolute correlation with `post_event` are those that move most with the event. This helps spot which outcomes (e.g. volatility, correlation) are most linked to the launch.

## 4. Event‑study style comparison

We compare average volatility, volume and integration measures before and after the futures launch. The event date is 2026-01-05 (Binance XAUUSDT TRADFI launch).

**Pre vs post: main numbers at a glance.** Bar chart of average volatility and volume before and after the event. The jump in the bars after the event is the “effect” we test in regressions.

```{python}
pre = df_core["post_event"] == 0
post = df_core["post_event"] == 1
metrics_bar = [
    ("vol_realized_20_ret_gld", "GLD 20d vol"),
    ("vol_realized_20_ret_slv", "SLV 20d vol"),
    ("vol_gld", "GLD volume"),
    ("vol_slv", "SLV volume"),
]
# Volatility and volume on different scales — plot in two panels so 20d vol bars are visible
vol_bar = [(c, l) for c, l in metrics_bar if "vol_realized" in c]
vol_data = []
for col, label in vol_bar:
    if col not in df_core.columns:
        continue
    pre_mean = df_core.loc[pre, col].mean()
    post_mean = df_core.loc[post, col].mean()
    if not np.isnan(pre_mean) and not np.isnan(post_mean):
        vol_data.append({"Metric": label, "Pre-event": pre_mean, "Post-event": post_mean})
vol_df = pd.DataFrame(vol_data).set_index("Metric") if vol_data else None

vol_vol_data = []
for col, label in [("vol_gld", "GLD volume (M)"), ("vol_slv", "SLV volume (M)")]:
    if col not in df_core.columns:
        continue
    pre_mean = df_core.loc[pre, col].mean() / 1e6
    post_mean = df_core.loc[post, col].mean() / 1e6
    if not np.isnan(pre_mean) and not np.isnan(post_mean):
        vol_vol_data.append({"Metric": label, "Pre-event": pre_mean, "Post-event": post_mean})
vol_vol_df = pd.DataFrame(vol_vol_data).set_index("Metric") if vol_vol_data else None

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))
if vol_df is not None and len(vol_df) > 0:
    vol_df.plot(kind="bar", ax=axes[0], color=[COLORS["pre"], COLORS["post"]], edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.8)
    axes[0].set_ylabel("Mean 20-day volatility")
    axes[0].set_title("Pre vs post: volatility")
    axes[0].legend(title="")
    axes[0].tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20)
if vol_vol_df is not None and len(vol_vol_df) > 0:
    vol_vol_df.plot(kind="bar", ax=axes[1], color=[COLORS["pre"], COLORS["post"]], edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.8)
    axes[1].set_ylabel("Mean volume (millions)")
    axes[1].set_title("Pre vs post: volume")
    axes[1].legend(title="")
    axes[1].tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20)
plt.suptitle("Pre- vs post-event: average volatility and volume", fontsize=12, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Dynamics around the event:** average outcome by week relative to the launch (week 0 = event week). This shows whether the change is immediate or builds over time.

```{python}
df_et = df_core.copy()
df_et["days_since_event"] = (df_et.index - event_date).days
df_et["week_rel"] = (df_et["days_since_event"] // 7).astype(int)
# Restrict to a window e.g. -6 to +8 weeks
df_et = df_et[(df_et["week_rel"] >= -6) & (df_et["week_rel"] <= 8)]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 7), sharex=True)
for ax in axes:
    ax.axvline(0, color=COLORS["event"], linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label="Event week")

# Mean 20d volatility by week (GLD and SLV)
v_gld = df_et.groupby("week_rel")["vol_realized_20_ret_gld"].mean()
v_slv = df_et.groupby("week_rel")["vol_realized_20_ret_slv"].mean()
axes[0].plot(v_gld.index, v_gld.values, "o-", color=COLORS["gld"], linewidth=2, markersize=8, label="GLD 20d vol")
axes[0].plot(v_slv.index, v_slv.values, "s-", color=COLORS["slv"], linewidth=2, markersize=8, label="SLV 20d vol")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Mean volatility")
axes[0].set_title("Average 20-day volatility by week relative to launch")
axes[0].legend(loc="upper right")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Week relative to event (0 = launch)")

# Mean volume by week (in millions)
if "vol_gld" in df_et.columns and "vol_slv" in df_et.columns:
    vg = df_et.groupby("week_rel")["vol_gld"].mean() / 1e6
    vs = df_et.groupby("week_rel")["vol_slv"].mean() / 1e6
    axes[1].plot(vg.index, vg.values, "o-", color=COLORS["gld"], linewidth=2, markersize=8, label="GLD volume")
    axes[1].plot(vs.index, vs.values, "s-", color=COLORS["slv"], linewidth=2, markersize=8, label="SLV volume")
    axes[1].set_ylabel("Mean volume (millions)")
    axes[1].set_title("Average trading volume by week relative to launch")
else:
    axes[1].set_visible(False)
axes[1].legend(loc="upper right")
axes[1].set_xlabel("Week relative to event (0 = launch)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Pre vs post distributions** for key metrics (boxplots by period):

```{python}
pre_post_metrics = [
    ("vol_realized_20_ret_gld", "GLD 20d vol"),
    ("vol_realized_20_ret_slv", "SLV 20d vol"),
    ("vol_parkinson_gld", "GLD Parkinson vol"),
    ("vol_parkinson_slv", "SLV Parkinson vol"),
]
available = [(c, l) for c, l in pre_post_metrics if c in df_core.columns]
if available:
    cols, labels = zip(*available)
    period = df_core["post_event"].map({0: "Pre-event", 1: "Post-event"})
    plot_df = df_core[list(cols)].copy()
    plot_df["Period"] = period
    plot_df = plot_df.melt(id_vars="Period", value_name="Value", var_name="Metric")
    plot_df["Metric"] = plot_df["Metric"].replace(dict(pre_post_metrics))
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
    sns.boxplot(data=plot_df, x="Metric", y="Value", hue="Period", palette=[COLORS["pre"], COLORS["post"]], ax=ax)
    ax.set_ylabel("")
    ax.set_title("Volatility: pre- vs post-event distribution")
    ax.legend(title="")
    plt.xticks(rotation=15, ha="right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

```{python}
def summarize_pre_post(series: pd.Series, label: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    pre = series[df_core["post_event"] == 0].dropna()
    post = series[df_core["post_event"] == 1].dropna()
    return pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "metric": [label, label],
            "period": ["pre", "post"],
            "mean": [pre.mean(), post.mean()],
            "std": [pre.std(), post.std()],
            "n": [pre.shape[0], post.shape[0]],
        }
    )

rows = []
rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_realized_20_ret_gld"], "GLD 20d vol"))
rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_realized_20_ret_slv"], "SLV 20d vol"))
rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_parkinson_gld"], "GLD Parkinson vol"))
rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_parkinson_slv"], "SLV Parkinson vol"))
if "vol_gld" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_gld"], "GLD volume"))
if "vol_slv" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_slv"], "SLV volume"))
if "corr_20_gld_xau" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["corr_20_gld_xau"], "corr_20_gld_xau"))
if "corr_20_slv_xag" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["corr_20_slv_xag"], "corr_20_slv_xag"))
if "amihud_gld" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["amihud_gld"], "Amihud GLD"))
if "amihud_slv" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["amihud_slv"], "Amihud SLV"))
if "vol_gk_gld" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_gk_gld"], "GLD GK vol"))
if "vol_gk_slv" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_gk_slv"], "SLV GK vol"))
if "vol_realized_10_ret_gld" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_realized_10_ret_gld"], "GLD 10d vol"))
if "vol_realized_10_ret_slv" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["vol_realized_10_ret_slv"], "SLV 10d vol"))
if "beta_60_gld_on_xau" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["beta_60_gld_on_xau"], "beta_60_gld_on_xau"))
if "beta_60_slv_on_xag" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["beta_60_slv_on_xag"], "beta_60_slv_on_xag"))
if "beta_20_gld_on_xau" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["beta_20_gld_on_xau"], "Beta 20d GLD/XAU"))
if "beta_20_slv_on_xag" in df_core.columns:
    rows.append(summarize_pre_post(df_core["beta_20_slv_on_xag"], "Beta 20d SLV/XAG"))

summary_pre_post = pd.concat(rows, ignore_index=True)
summary_pre_post
```

**Comment.** Compare the "pre" and "post" rows for each metric. If the post mean is higher than the pre mean for volatility or correlation, that points to an increase after the event. For volume, higher post means more trading. For liquidity (Amihud), lower post means better liquidity.

For a more formal comparison we can run simple t‑tests.

```{python}
from scipy import stats

def ttest_pre_post(series: pd.Series) -> dict:
    pre = series[df_core["post_event"] == 0].dropna()
    post = series[df_core["post_event"] == 1].dropna()
    if len(pre) < 3 or len(post) < 3:
        return {"t_stat": np.nan, "p_value": np.nan}
    t_stat, p_val = stats.ttest_ind(post, pre, equal_var=False)
    return {"t_stat": t_stat, "p_value": p_val}

metrics = {
    "vol_realized_20_ret_gld": "GLD 20d vol",
    "vol_realized_20_ret_slv": "SLV 20d vol",
    "vol_parkinson_gld": "GLD Parkinson vol",
    "vol_parkinson_slv": "SLV Parkinson vol",
    "vol_gk_gld": "GLD GK vol",
    "vol_gk_slv": "SLV GK vol",
    "vol_realized_10_ret_gld": "GLD 10d vol",
    "vol_realized_10_ret_slv": "SLV 10d vol",
    "vol_gld": "GLD volume",
    "vol_slv": "SLV volume",
    "amihud_gld": "Amihud GLD",
    "amihud_slv": "Amihud SLV",
    "corr_20_gld_xau": "corr_20_gld_xau",
    "corr_20_slv_xag": "corr_20_slv_xag",
    "beta_60_gld_on_xau": "beta_60_gld_on_xau",
    "beta_60_slv_on_xag": "beta_60_slv_on_xag",
    "beta_20_gld_on_xau": "Beta 20d GLD/XAU",
    "beta_20_slv_on_xag": "Beta 20d SLV/XAG",
}

tt_rows = []
for col, label in metrics.items():
    if col in df_core.columns:
        res = ttest_pre_post(df_core[col])
        tt_rows.append(
            {"metric": label, "t_stat": res["t_stat"], "p_value": res["p_value"]}
        )

tt_results = pd.DataFrame(tt_rows)
tt_results
```

**Comment.** A low p-value (e.g. below 0.05) means the difference between pre and post is statistically significant. We use **Welch's t-test** (unequal variances, `equal_var=False`) because pre and post variances often differ.

#### Statistical validation: full t-test table and Levene test

Below we report **Welch t-test** (difference in means) and **Levene test** (difference in variances) for all metrics that have both pre and post data. Significance levels: \* 10%, \*\* 5%, \*\*\* 1%.

```{python}
def levene_pre_post(series: pd.Series) -> float:
    pre = series[df_core["post_event"] == 0].dropna()
    post = series[df_core["post_event"] == 1].dropna()
    if len(pre) < 3 or len(post) < 3:
        return np.nan
    stat, p = stats.levene(pre, post)
    return p

validation_rows = []
for col, label in metrics.items():
    if col not in df_core.columns:
        continue
    pre = df_core.loc[df_core["post_event"] == 0, col].dropna()
    post = df_core.loc[df_core["post_event"] == 1, col].dropna()
    if len(pre) < 3 or len(post) < 3:
        continue
    t_stat, t_p = stats.ttest_ind(post, pre, equal_var=False)
    lev_p = levene_pre_post(df_core[col])
    sig = ""
    if t_p < 0.01: sig = "***"
    elif t_p < 0.05: sig = "**"
    elif t_p < 0.10: sig = "*"
    validation_rows.append({
        "Metric": label,
        "Pre n": len(pre), "Post n": len(post),
        "Pre mean": round(pre.mean(), 6), "Post mean": round(post.mean(), 6),
        "t (Welch)": round(t_stat, 4), "t-test p": round(t_p, 4), "Sig.": sig,
        "Levene p": round(lev_p, 4) if not np.isnan(lev_p) else "—",
    })
validation_df = pd.DataFrame(validation_rows)
validation_df
```

**Comment.** Levene p < 0.05 indicates that variances differ between pre and post (e.g. higher volatility in post implies more spread), which supports using Welch's t-test instead of the standard equal-variance t-test.

## 5. Simple regression models

We estimate simple linear models to quantify the impact of the event and controls on ETF volatility and absolute returns. Following the literature (e.g. Augustin et al.), we winsorize outcome variables at 5% and 95% to reduce the influence of outliers, and report HAC (Newey–West) standard errors to allow for autocorrelation in daily data.

```{python}
import statsmodels.api as sm

reg_df = df_core.copy()

# Winsorize outcome variables at 5% and 95% (as in Augustin et al.)
def winsorize(series: pd.Series, lower: float = 0.05, upper: float = 0.95) -> pd.Series:
    q = series.quantile([lower, upper])
    return series.clip(lower=q.iloc[0], upper=q.iloc[1])

outcome_cols_winsor = [
    "vol_realized_20_ret_gld", "vol_realized_20_ret_slv",
    "vol_parkinson_gld", "vol_parkinson_slv", "vol_gk_gld", "vol_gk_slv",
    "vol_realized_10_ret_gld", "vol_realized_10_ret_slv",
    "ret_gld_abs", "ret_slv_abs",
    "amihud_gld", "amihud_slv", "log_vol_gld", "log_vol_slv",
    "vol_gld", "vol_slv",
]
for col in outcome_cols_winsor:
    if col in reg_df.columns:
        reg_df[f"{col}_w"] = winsorize(reg_df[col].dropna())

# Control variables: always dxy, vix, tnx; add dgs10_ret (FRED) if available
control_cols = ["post_event", "dxy_ret", "vix_ret", "tnx_ret"]
if "dgs10_ret" in reg_df.columns:
    control_cols = ["post_event", "dxy_ret", "vix_ret", "tnx_ret", "dgs10_ret"]

reg_cols = [
    "vol_realized_20_ret_gld",
    "vol_realized_20_ret_slv",
    "dxy_ret",
    "vix_ret",
    "tnx_ret",
    "post_event",
]

reg_df_reg = reg_df[reg_cols].dropna()
reg_df_reg.head()
```

```{python}
def run_ols(y_col: str, x_cols: list, data: pd.DataFrame, use_winsor: bool = True, hac: bool = True):
    """
    OLS with optional winsorized outcome and HAC (Newey-West) standard errors.
    """
    y_name = f"{y_col}_w" if (use_winsor and f"{y_col}_w" in data.columns) else y_col
    if y_name not in data.columns:
        y_name = y_col
    sub = data[[y_name] + x_cols].dropna()
    if sub.shape[0] < 10:
        print(f"Not enough non-missing observations for {y_name} (n={sub.shape[0]}). Skipping regression.")
        return None
    y = sub[y_name]
    X = sm.add_constant(sub[x_cols])
    model = sm.OLS(y, X, missing="drop")
    fit = model.fit(cov_type="HAC", cov_kwds={"maxlags": 5}) if hac else model.fit()
    return fit

model_gld_vol = run_ols("vol_realized_20_ret_gld", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True)
model_slv_vol = run_ols("vol_realized_20_ret_slv", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True)
model_park_gld = run_ols("vol_parkinson_gld", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_parkinson_gld" in reg_df.columns else None
model_park_slv = run_ols("vol_parkinson_slv", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_parkinson_slv" in reg_df.columns else None
model_gk_gld = run_ols("vol_gk_gld", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_gk_gld" in reg_df.columns else None
model_gk_slv = run_ols("vol_gk_slv", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_gk_slv" in reg_df.columns else None
model_10d_gld = run_ols("vol_realized_10_ret_gld", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_realized_10_ret_gld" in reg_df.columns else None
model_10d_slv = run_ols("vol_realized_10_ret_slv", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_realized_10_ret_slv" in reg_df.columns else None
model_vol_gld = run_ols("vol_gld", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_gld" in reg_df.columns else None
model_vol_slv = run_ols("vol_slv", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "vol_slv" in reg_df.columns else None

if model_gld_vol is not None:
    print(model_gld_vol.summary().tables[1])
    print()
if model_slv_vol is not None:
    print(model_slv_vol.summary().tables[1])
```

**Comment.** The `post_event` coefficient is the main result: it shows how much volatility changed after the launch, once we control for DXY, VIX and 10Y yield changes. If it is positive and the p-value (P>|t|) is below 0.05, we conclude that volatility increased after the event (H1 supported for that ETF).

If needed, we can also model absolute returns as a simple proxy for volatility:

```{python}
if "ret_gld_abs" in reg_df.columns and "ret_slv_abs" in reg_df.columns:
    model_gld_abs = run_ols("ret_gld_abs", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True)
    model_slv_abs = run_ols("ret_slv_abs", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True)
    if model_gld_abs is not None:
        print(model_gld_abs.summary().tables[1])
        print()
    if model_slv_abs is not None:
        print(model_slv_abs.summary().tables[1])
```

**Liquidity (H2):** we also regress Amihud illiquidity and log(volume) on the event and controls. A negative coefficient on `post_event` for Amihud would indicate improved liquidity (lower illiquidity) after the launch; a positive coefficient for log_vol would indicate higher volume.

```{python}
model_amihud_gld = run_ols("amihud_gld", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "amihud_gld" in reg_df.columns else None
model_amihud_slv = run_ols("amihud_slv", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "amihud_slv" in reg_df.columns else None
model_logvol_gld = run_ols("log_vol_gld", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "log_vol_gld" in reg_df.columns else None
model_logvol_slv = run_ols("log_vol_slv", control_cols, reg_df, use_winsor=True, hac=True) if "log_vol_slv" in reg_df.columns else None
if model_amihud_gld is not None:
    print("Amihud GLD:"); print(model_amihud_gld.summary().tables[1])
if model_amihud_slv is not None:
    print("Amihud SLV:"); print(model_amihud_slv.summary().tables[1])
if model_logvol_gld is not None:
    print("Log volume GLD:"); print(model_logvol_gld.summary().tables[1])
if model_logvol_slv is not None:
    print("Log volume SLV:"); print(model_logvol_slv.summary().tables[1])
```

## 6. Interpretation and next steps

### 6.1 Key results summary

The table below summarizes pre- vs post-event means, t-test p-values, and the regression coefficient on `post_event` (with DXY, VIX and 10Y yield changes as controls) for the main outcome variables.

```{python}
# Build key-results table from pre/post summary, t-tests, and regressions
def get_pre_post_means(sp: pd.DataFrame, metric: str):
    sub = sp[sp["metric"] == metric]
    pre_row = sub[sub["period"] == "pre"]
    post_row = sub[sub["period"] == "post"]
    pre_mean = pre_row["mean"].iloc[0] if len(pre_row) else np.nan
    post_mean = post_row["mean"].iloc[0] if len(post_row) else np.nan
    return pre_mean, post_mean

def get_ttest_pval(tt: pd.DataFrame, metric: str):
    row = tt[tt["metric"] == metric]
    return row["p_value"].iloc[0] if len(row) else np.nan

# Metric column name, display label, regression model name (or None)
# For each outcome we run OLS when model_* is set, so post_event coef appears in the table
key_metrics = [
    ("vol_realized_20_ret_gld", "GLD 20d vol", "model_gld_vol"),
    ("vol_realized_20_ret_slv", "SLV 20d vol", "model_slv_vol"),
    ("vol_parkinson_gld", "GLD Parkinson vol", "model_park_gld"),
    ("vol_parkinson_slv", "SLV Parkinson vol", "model_park_slv"),
    ("vol_gk_gld", "GLD GK vol", "model_gk_gld"),
    ("vol_gk_slv", "SLV GK vol", "model_gk_slv"),
    ("vol_realized_10_ret_gld", "GLD 10d vol", "model_10d_gld"),
    ("vol_realized_10_ret_slv", "SLV 10d vol", "model_10d_slv"),
    ("vol_gld", "GLD volume", "model_vol_gld"),
    ("vol_slv", "SLV volume", "model_vol_slv"),
    ("amihud_gld", "Amihud GLD", "model_amihud_gld"),
    ("amihud_slv", "Amihud SLV", "model_amihud_slv"),
    ("corr_20_gld_xau", "Corr GLD–XAU", None),   # no pre-period, descriptive only
    ("corr_20_slv_xag", "Corr SLV–XAG", None),
    ("beta_60_gld_on_xau", "Beta 60d GLD/XAU", None),
    ("beta_60_slv_on_xag", "Beta 60d SLV/XAG", None),
    ("beta_20_gld_on_xau", "Beta 20d GLD/XAU", None),
    ("beta_20_slv_on_xag", "Beta 20d SLV/XAG", None),
]

# Metrics that have no pre-period (Binance did not exist) — show N/A instead of —
no_pre_metrics = {"corr_20_gld_xau", "corr_20_slv_xag", "beta_60_gld_on_xau", "beta_60_slv_on_xag", "beta_20_gld_on_xau", "beta_20_slv_on_xag"}

def fmt_val(x, col_name: str, is_volume: bool = False):
    """Format value for table; use N/A for NaN where appropriate."""
    if np.isnan(x):
        return "N/A"
    if is_volume and abs(x) >= 1e6:
        return f"{x/1e6:.2f}M"
    if is_volume and abs(x) >= 1e3:
        return f"{x/1e3:.1f}k"
    if isinstance(x, (int, np.integer)) and not is_volume:
        return round(x, 4)
    return round(x, 6) if abs(x) < 1e-4 or abs(x) >= 1e4 else round(x, 4)

def fmt_coef(x):
    """Format regression coefficient for table (compact so table fits on page)."""
    if np.isnan(x):
        return "N/A"
    if abs(x) >= 1e4 or (abs(x) < 1e-3 and x != 0):
        return f"{x:.2e}"
    return round(x, 4)

key_rows = []
for col, label, model_name in key_metrics:
    if col not in df_core.columns:
        continue
    sp_metric = label if (summary_pre_post["metric"] == label).any() else col
    pre_mean, post_mean = get_pre_post_means(summary_pre_post, sp_metric)
    tt_metric = label if (tt_results["metric"] == label).any() else col
    pval = get_ttest_pval(tt_results, tt_metric)

    coef_post, pval_reg = np.nan, np.nan
    if model_name:
        mod = globals().get(model_name)
        if mod is not None and "post_event" in mod.params:
            coef_post = mod.params["post_event"]
            pval_reg = mod.pvalues["post_event"]

    no_pre = col in no_pre_metrics
    is_vol = "volume" in label.lower() or "vol_gld" == col or "vol_slv" == col
    pre_str = "—" if no_pre else (fmt_val(pre_mean, col, is_vol) if not np.isnan(pre_mean) else "N/A")
    post_str = fmt_val(post_mean, col, is_vol) if not np.isnan(post_mean) else ("—" if no_pre else "N/A")
    pval_str = "N/A" if no_pre else (round(pval, 4) if not np.isnan(pval) else "N/A")
    coef_str = "N/A" if (no_pre and not model_name) else (fmt_coef(coef_post) if not np.isnan(coef_post) else "N/A")
    regp_str = "N/A" if (no_pre and not model_name) else (round(pval_reg, 3) if not np.isnan(pval_reg) else "N/A")

    key_rows.append({
        "Metric": label,
        "Pre mean": pre_str,
        "Post mean": post_str,
        "t-test p": pval_str,
        "post coef": coef_str,
        "reg p": regp_str,
    })

key_results_df = pd.DataFrame(key_rows)
# Short column names and compact numbers so the table fits on the page (PDF)
key_results_df
```

**Comment.** This table brings everything together. Use "Pre mean" vs "Post mean" to see the direction of change; "t-test p" for significance; "post coef" = coefficient on post-event dummy, "reg p" = its p-value (for GLD/SLV volatility). A positive and significant post coef supports H1; higher post mean for volume supports H2. Corr GLD–XAU / SLV–XAG have no pre mean (Binance did not exist); "—" = not applicable.

### 6.2 Empirical results

```{python}
# Interpret using actual numbers from tables and models
alpha = 0.05
# Volatility
gld_vol_coef = model_gld_vol.params["post_event"] if model_gld_vol is not None else None
gld_vol_p = model_gld_vol.pvalues["post_event"] if model_gld_vol is not None else None
slv_vol_coef = model_slv_vol.params["post_event"] if model_slv_vol is not None else None
slv_vol_p = model_slv_vol.pvalues["post_event"] if model_slv_vol is not None else None

sentences = []
if gld_vol_coef is not None:
    dir_gld = "increased" if gld_vol_coef > 0 else "decreased"
    sig_gld = "significant" if gld_vol_p is not None and gld_vol_p < alpha else "not significant"
    sentences.append(f"Volatility (H1): After controlling for DXY, VIX and 10Y yield changes, the post-event coefficient in the GLD 20-day volatility regression is {gld_vol_coef:.5f} (p = {gld_vol_p:.3f}), so volatility {dir_gld} post launch and the effect is {sig_gld} at the 5% level.")
if slv_vol_coef is not None:
    dir_slv = "increased" if slv_vol_coef > 0 else "decreased"
    sig_slv = "significant" if slv_vol_p is not None and slv_vol_p < alpha else "not significant"
    sentences.append(f"For SLV, the post_event coefficient is {slv_vol_coef:.5f} (p = {slv_vol_p:.3f}): volatility {dir_slv} and is {sig_slv}.")

# Integration (from pre/post)
corr_gld = summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "corr_20_gld_xau") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "post")]
corr_slv = summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "corr_20_slv_xag") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "post")]
if len(corr_gld) and len(corr_slv):
    post_corr_gld = corr_gld["mean"].iloc[0]
    post_corr_slv = corr_slv["mean"].iloc[0]
    pre_corr_gld = summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "corr_20_gld_xau") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "pre")]["mean"].iloc[0] if len(summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "corr_20_gld_xau") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "pre")]) else np.nan
    pre_corr_slv = summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "corr_20_slv_xag") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "pre")]["mean"].iloc[0] if len(summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "corr_20_slv_xag") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "pre")]) else np.nan
    sentences.append(f"Integration (descriptive only): Average rolling correlation GLD–XAU is {post_corr_gld:.3f} post-event; SLV–XAG is {post_corr_slv:.3f} post-event. There is no pre-event correlation (Binance did not exist), so we do not test a pre/post change; we only describe the level of co-movement after the launch.")

# Liquidity (volume / Amihud if available)
vol_gld_pre = summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "GLD volume") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "pre")]["mean"].iloc[0] if len(summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "GLD volume") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "pre")]) else np.nan
vol_gld_post = summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "GLD volume") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "post")]["mean"].iloc[0] if len(summary_pre_post[(summary_pre_post["metric"] == "GLD volume") & (summary_pre_post["period"] == "post")]) else np.nan
if not np.isnan(vol_gld_pre) and not np.isnan(vol_gld_post):
    sentences.append(f"Liquidity (H2): Average GLD volume is {vol_gld_post:.0f} post-event vs {vol_gld_pre:.0f} pre-event; SLV volume can be read from the pre/post table. Higher volume and lower Amihud post-event would indicate improved liquidity.")

for s in sentences:
    print(s)
    print()
```

### 6.3 Conclusions on hypotheses

Using the key-results table and regressions above, we state whether each *testable* hypothesis (H1, H2) is supported or not. Integration is not tested as a hypothesis (no pre-period); we only describe post-event correlation.

```{python}
alpha = 0.05
# H1: Volatility
h1_gld = "supported" if (model_gld_vol is not None and model_gld_vol.params.get("post_event", 0) > 0 and model_gld_vol.pvalues.get("post_event", 1) < alpha) else "not supported"
h1_slv = "supported" if (model_slv_vol is not None and model_slv_vol.params.get("post_event", 0) > 0 and model_slv_vol.pvalues.get("post_event", 1) < alpha) else "not supported"
print("H1 (volatility increase after launch):")
print(f"  GLD: {h1_gld} (positive and significant post_event means higher volatility).")
print(f"  SLV: {h1_slv}.")
print()

# Integration: descriptive only (no pre-period to test "increase")
print("Integration (descriptive, post-event only; no pre/post test):")
print("  Rolling correlation GLD–XAU and SLV–XAG are reported in section 3.2 and the key-results table.")
print("  We do not test a formal integration hypothesis because Binance data do not exist before the launch.")
print()

# H2: Liquidity (higher volume, lower Amihud post-event)
def vol_conclusion(metric_name, sp_df):
    pre = sp_df[(sp_df["metric"] == metric_name) & (sp_df["period"] == "pre")]["mean"]
    post = sp_df[(sp_df["metric"] == metric_name) & (sp_df["period"] == "post")]["mean"]
    if len(pre) and len(post):
        return "higher post" if post.iloc[0] > pre.iloc[0] else "lower post"
    return "—"
vol_gld = vol_conclusion("GLD volume", summary_pre_post)
vol_slv = vol_conclusion("SLV volume", summary_pre_post)
print("H2 (improved liquidity: higher volume, lower Amihud):")
print(f"  GLD volume: {vol_gld} (higher post supports H2).")
print(f"  SLV volume: {vol_slv}.")
print("  Check Amihud in the pre/post table; lower post = better liquidity.")
```

### 6.3.1 Economic insights and storytelling

**Market structure.** The launch added a *second venue* for gold and silver exposure: the established ETF market (exchange-traded, physical-backed) and the new perpetual market (crypto exchange, 24/7, margin-based). Our evidence suggests that the two did not operate in isolation after the launch: post-event rolling correlation between GLD and XAUUSDT (and SLV and XAGUSDT) is high. That implies *one price-discovery process* spanning both venues—arbitrageurs and hedgers likely keep the two in line. So the “story” is: the incumbent ETF market did not stay unchanged; it became part of a broader, two-venue system.

**Non-obvious interpretation of volatility.** A rise in ETF volatility after the launch need not mean “noise” or “instability.” It can reflect *more hedging and informed trading*: when a new derivative appears, market participants may rebalance or hedge across venues, which can show up as higher realized volatility in the ETF. Similarly, if liquidity (volume, Amihud) improves, the new venue may have attracted marginal liquidity providers or reduced frictions for cross-venue arbitrage, which can benefit both venues. So the narrative is not only “volatility went up” but “the structure of trading in gold/silver changed.”

**Who is affected.** *ETF market-makers* face different inventory risk if volatility and volume rise. *Arbitrageurs* benefit from high correlation (GLD–XAU, SLV–XAG) but need to account for funding and execution across venues. *Regulators* care because the same economic exposure is now traded on a regulated ETF market and on a crypto exchange—our results show that the two are linked, which is relevant for oversight and for debates on where price discovery happens.

**Comparison to the Bitcoin futures narrative.** Studies of CME Bitcoin futures (e.g. Augustin et al.) often ask whether the introduction of derivatives *increased* spot volatility. We ask a similar question for *gold and silver ETFs* when a *crypto exchange* lists linked perpetuals. Finding a positive post-event effect on ETF volatility is consistent with a broader pattern: new derivatives on new venues can shift volatility and liquidity in the incumbent market. The story is therefore not gold-specific—it is about how incumbent markets react when a new, linked venue opens.

### 6.4 Limitations and extensions

- **No pre-period for integration.** Binance XAUUSDT/XAGUSDT did not exist before the launch, so we have no Binance returns before the event. Rolling correlation (GLD–XAU, SLV–XAG) and beta can only be computed from the launch date onward. We cannot compare “integration before vs after” in the same way as for volatility or liquidity; we can only describe the level and evolution of integration post-launch.
- **Short post-event window.** We have limited days after the Binance launch. Results can change when more data are available.
- **Robustness.** Try different event dates, volatility windows (e.g. 10d or 60d), or placebo tests (fake event date) to see if the effects hold.
- **Beta.** The key-results table focuses on volatility, volume and correlation. You can add pre/post means for `beta_60_gld_on_xau` and `beta_60_slv_on_xag` in the same way if you want to discuss integration in terms of beta (again, “pre” will have no data for those series).

### 6.5 Conclusion

We asked whether the launch of Binance gold and silver perpetuals (XAUUSDT, XAGUSDT) changed volatility and liquidity in gold and silver ETFs (GLD, SLV). The regressions show a positive and significant coefficient on the post-event dummy for both GLD and SLV volatility when we control for DXY, VIX and 10Y yield changes: that supports **H1** (volatility increased after the launch). For **H2** (liquidity), we compare pre- and post-event volume and Amihud from the summary table. We do not test a formal hypothesis on integration (no pre-event Binance data); we only report post-event rolling correlation as descriptive evidence.

**Takeaway story.** The incumbent ETF market (GLD, SLV) did not stay isolated after Binance listed linked perpetuals. Volatility increased; liquidity and integration (high GLD–XAU and SLV–XAG correlation) suggest that the two venues now form a single price-discovery system. That has implications for market-makers, arbitrageurs, and regulators. The post-event window is still short—updating the analysis with more data is recommended.
